perturbation.original_text: The term Confidential Information also includes, without limitation: (a) the identity (by name or identifiable description) of the parties hereto; (b) the fact that the parties hereto are considering a Transaction; and (c) all analyses, compilations, forecasts, summaries, studies or other materials prepared by the Receiving Party and its Representatives (as defined below) in connection with their review of, or the Receiving Party’s interest in, the Transaction which, in whole or in part, contain or reflect or are based on any information referred to in this Section (“ Analyses ”).
perturbation.changed_text: The term Confidential Information also might include: (a) the identity (potentially by name or possibly identifiable description) of the parties hereto; (b) the fact that the parties hereto may be considering a Transaction; and (c) any analyses, compilations, forecasts, summaries, studies or other materials prepared by the Receiving Party and its Representatives (as defined below) in connection with their review of, or the Receiving Party’s interest in, the Transaction which, in part, appear to contain or reflect or are loosely based on some information referred to in this Section (“ Analyses ”).
perturbation.explanation: By adding qualifiers such as 'might include', 'potentially', 'possibly', 'may be considering', 'any' and 'loosely based', the scope of what constitutes Confidential Information is made significantly more vague. This could hinder the enforcement of confidentiality obligations, as it creates room for argument about whether specific information falls under the definition. It introduces multiple interpretations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as referenced in the original contract)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires specific identification and protection of trade secrets. By making the definition of 'Confidential Information' vague, it undermines the ability to prove that certain information qualifies as a trade secret under the Act. Under the UTSA, Trade Secret requires that the owner has taken measures to protect the information.

Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguities that weaken the protection of confidential information, potentially undermining its status as a trade secret. The original text provides a more definitive scope of what constitutes Confidential Information, whereas the changed text introduces uncertainty. The Law Explanation states that the Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires specific identification and protection of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Trade secret' as information that 'Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known'. By making the definition of 'Confidential Information' vague, it becomes harder to prove the information's value and secrecy, directly contradicting the requirements for trade secret protection, and hindering enforcement.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that for a period of two (2) years from the date of this Agreement, neither it nor any of its Representatives will, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment or employ any individual serving as an officer of the Disclosing Party or any employee of the Disclosing Party or any of its subsidiaries, in each case with whom the Receiving Party has had substantial contact during its investigation of the Disclosing Party and its business, in each case without obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party; provided that the Receiving Party may make general solicitations for employment not specifically directed at the Disclosing Party or any of its subsidiaries or their respective employees (including, without limitation, by a bona fide search firm) and solicit and employ (i) any person who responds to such general solicitations, and (ii) any person with whom the Receiving Party was discussing employment prior to the date of this Agreement or with whom the Receiving Party initiates discussions regarding employment after such person is no longer an employee of the Disclosing Party or its subsidiaries.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that for a period of two (2) years from the date of this Agreement, neither it nor any of its Representatives will, directly or indirectly, actively solicit for employment or actually employ any individual serving as an officer of the Disclosing Party or any employee of the Disclosing Party or any of its subsidiaries, in each case with whom the Receiving Party has had what could be deemed as substantial contact during its investigation of the Disclosing Party and its business, without obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party; provided that the Receiving Party may make general solicitations for employment not specifically directed at the Disclosing Party or any of its subsidiaries or their respective employees (including, without limitation, by a bona fide search firm) and solicit and employ (i) any person who responds to such general solicitations, and (ii) any person with whom the Receiving Party was discussing employment prior to the date of this Agreement or with whom the Receiving Party initiates discussions regarding employment after such person is no longer an employee of the Disclosing Party or its subsidiaries.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing 'actively solicit' and 'actually employ' raises the bar, potentially allowing subtle solicitation that isn't deemed 'active.' Also, the phrase 'what could be deemed as substantial contact' introduces subjectivity, making it difficult to determine whether the no-solicit clause applies. This uncertainty diminishes the protection against employee poaching.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law allows for reasonable restrictions on solicitation of employees, but only if they are unambiguously defined. By introducing ambiguity into the definition of 'substantial contact' and 'actively solicit', the modification makes it more difficult to enforce the non-solicitation agreement under Delaware law. Courts will look to the reasonableness of the restrictions.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding 'substantial contact' and uses 'actively solicit' instead of 'solicit', weakening the enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement. The Law Explanation states that Delaware law requires reasonable restrictions on solicitation that are unambiguously defined. The introduction of subjective terms makes it more difficult to enforce the non-solicitation agreement under Delaware law.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information on a need to know basis to its and its affiliates’ officers, directors, employees, consultants and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, investment banks, the agents and lenders under the Receiving Party’s existing credit facilities, attorneys and accountants) (“ Representatives ”) who have a need to know such information for purposes of evaluation and completion of the Transaction; provided that such Representatives shall be bound by terms of confidentiality and non-use consistent with those set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may, at their discretion, disclose Confidential Information on what they perceive is a need to know basis to its and its affiliates’ officers, directors, employees, consultants and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, investment banks, the agents and lenders under the Receiving Party’s existing credit facilities, attorneys and accountants) (“ Representatives ”) who might arguably have a need to know such information for purposes of possible evaluation and hypothetical completion of the Transaction; provided that such Representatives should ideally be bound by terms of confidentiality and non-use generally consistent with those set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'at their discretion', 'what they perceive', 'might arguably have', 'possible evaluation' and 'hypothetical completion' introduces substantial ambiguity into the disclosure clause. Replacing 'shall be bound' with 'should ideally be bound' weakens the requirement that representatives must adhere to the confidentiality terms. This gives the receiving party leeway to disclose information more freely, with a lessened burden to make their representatives maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Trade Secret Law - Duty to Protect
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware trade secret law emphasizes the need for reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By giving the Receiving Party broad discretion in disclosing information and weakening the requirement that Representatives be bound by confidentiality terms, the modification could be construed as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets under Delaware law. The change introduces uncertainty to the requirements set forth in the agreement.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity into the disclosure clause and weakens the requirement that representatives must adhere to the confidentiality terms. The Law Explanation supports the idea that this change could be construed as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets under Delaware law. The addition of discretionary language and weakening of the 'shall be bound' clause contradict the intent of protecting confidential information. Since ambiguities introduce difficulties for the contract to be enforced under state or national law and it risks non-compliance, a contradiction exists.
